questions.question: Which macromolecule carries genetic information and is composed of nucleotides?
questions.explanation: Nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) carry genetic information. DNA's nucleotides contain deoxyribose sugar + phosphate + one of four bases (A, T, G, C). RNA uses ribose and uracil instead of thymine.

questions.question: Enzymes are consumed (used up) during the reactions they catalyze.
questions.explanation: Enzymes are biological catalysts — they lower activation energy but are NOT consumed. They return to their original state after each catalytic cycle and can process thousands of substrate molecules per second.

questions.question: The process by which mRNA is used to build a protein is called ___.
questions.explanation: DNA → mRNA (transcription) → Protein (translation). Translation occurs on ribosomes: tRNA brings amino acids matched to mRNA codons. Each 3-nucleotide codon specifies one amino acid.

questions.question: Match each biomolecule to its primary function:
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin: O₂ carrier (iron-containing protein). Insulin: lowers blood sugar (peptide hormone). ATP: energy transfer (nucleotide). Phospholipids: bilayer membranes (amphipathic lipids).

questions.question: Arrange the stages of cellular respiration in correct order:
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: Glucose → Glycolysis (2 ATP, cytoplasm) → Pyruvate → Acetyl-CoA → Krebs cycle (2 ATP, NADH) → ETC (34 ATP, inner mitochondrial membrane). Total ≈ 36-38 ATP per glucose.

questions.question: A competitive enzyme inhibitor increases Km from 5mM to 15mM. If substrate concentration is 10mM, what fraction of Vmax is achieved? (Use Michaelis-Menten)
questions.explanation: With inhibitor: Km_app = 15mM. v/Vmax = 10/(15+10) = 10/25 = 0.40 (40% of Vmax). Without inhibitor: 10/(5+10) = 0.67. The competitive inhibitor reduced activity from 67% to 40% at this substrate concentration.

questions.question: Should CRISPR gene editing be used to enhance human embryos beyond disease prevention (e.g., intelligence, athleticism)?
questions.scenario: CRISPR can correct disease-causing mutations (sickle cell, cystic fibrosis). But the same technology could theoretically enhance non-disease traits. He Jiankui edited embryos (2018) — universally condemned.

options.label: Only for serious genetic diseases — draw a clear therapeutic line
options.framework: Medical necessity — cure disease, don't enhance

options.label: Allow all modifications — parents already select for traits (education, nutrition)
Ban all germline editing — risks to future generations are unacceptable
options.framework: Precautionary — don't modify what we can't undo

options.label: Allow disease correction now; revisit enhancement after safety data (decades of research)

questions.explanation: Scientific consensus: somatic gene therapy is acceptable; germline editing for serious disease needs more safety data; enhancement is premature and ethically fraught. The staged approach allows progress while maintaining safety standards.
